text:
Admission Date: 2002-06-12        Discharge Date: 1992-03-11

Date of Birth:  2012-04-10        Sex:  F

Service:  Medical ICU


HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  This is a 34-year-old patient
with type I insulin dependent diabetes and a history of
frequent admissions for diabetic ketoacidosis secondary to
medication noncompliance, who presented to the emergency
department on 02-21 with an acute onset of nausea and
vomiting.

The symptoms began at approximately 3 p.m. on the day of
arrival and involved one episode of coffee-ground emesis. The
patient initially complained to the emergency department
staff of some chest pressure but denied this at the time of
unit evaluation. She claimed she has been fully compliant
with her insulin regimen.

In the emergency department, the patient was given an
antiemetic for symptomatic control and started on an insulin
drip given her initial anion gap of 23. She denied any fever,
shaking chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, or
abdominal pain.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  Insulin dependent diabetes complicated
by neuropathy, nephropathy, retinopathy. History of
gastroparesis. Chronic renal insufficiency. Hypertension.
Asthma. Right foot ulcer. History of C-diff in the past.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:  Lantus and Humalog Insulin, Protonix,
Phenergan, atenolol.

ALLERGIES:  Aspirin, codeine, peanuts, Compazine.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  Vital signs: Initial vital signs
showed a blood pressure of 146/76, heart rate 90, respiratory
rate 14. General: The patient was drowsy but did not appear
in any acute distress. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic.
Pupils equal, round and reactive to light and accommodation.
Intact extraocular motions and visual fields. Mucous
membranes were noted to be dry. Neck: No elevation of jugular
venous pressure. Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No
wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Heart: Regular with a 2/6
systolic ejection murmur best noted at the right upper
sternal border. Abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended.
Somewhat decreased bowel sounds. Extremities: There was no
extremity edema. The left foot had a deep ulcer with a clean
bed and no evidence of purulent drainage.

LABORATORY DATA:  Complete blood count showed a white count
of 10.7, hematocrit 33.2, platelet count 579; chemistries
showed a sodium of 142, potassium 3.6, chloride 102, CO2 17,
BUN 51, creatinine 4.2, glucose 248, this added to an anion
gap of 23; first set of enzymes were sent which showed a CK
of 14, MB not performed, and a troponin of less than 0.01.

electrocardiogram in the emergency department showed sinus
rhythm at a rate of 93 with normal axis. Intervals were as
follows: PR 132, QRS 90, QTC 464. LVH was noted, but there
were no ischemic ST-T changes.

Chest x-ray was performed in the emergency department. AP
image showed no evidence of pulmonary infiltrates or
effusions.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  The patient was admitted to the medical
intensive care unit for management of her diabetic
ketoacidosis. As in similar episodes, the likely etiology of
this DK episode was poor compliance. No evidence of an
infectious or cardiac etiology was discovered on work-up.

She was placed on an insulin drip, and the gap closed with
this. The patient was seen by Hicks on hospital stay day #2,
07-26, for further evaluation of her diabetic care. They
commented on the increasing degree of renal dysfunction, and
at the rate of current decline, the patient would likely
require dialysis within the next year. They recommended a
renal evaluation at Hicks as an outpatient.

The largest barrier to making the patient feel better, was
recurrent nausea and vomiting from relatively severe diabetic
gastroparesis. The patient described the presence of some
coffee-ground emesis but repeatedly refused GI evaluation.
She was given multiple antiemetics including prokinetic
agents to assist motility and improve symptoms. Her
hematocrit was serially evaluated to look for evidence of
significant GI bleed.

The serial hematocrits showed a 4-point drop concomitant with
significant IV rehydration. The drop was assumed to be
related to hemodilution. She had been given 7-8 L of fluid
over the course of her stay. She never became hemodynamically
unstable while being observed on the unit.

The patient, by hospital day 4, was not taking full p.o. but
felt better enough that she wanted to discharge herself from
the hospital. The team discussed the risks of leaving
including recurrence of diabetic ketoacidosis, but the
patient insisted that she be allowed to go home.

She was discharged against medical advice and was set up with
a primary care physician appointment the day after discharge.
She was also given an appointment at Hicks with a renal
specialist to discuss the possibility of beginning dialysis.

CONDITION ON DISCHARGE:  Stable. Discharged to home.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
1. Diabetic ketoacidosis.
2. Gastroparesis.
3. Worsening chronic renal failure.


DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:  Atenolol 100 mg 1 tab daily, Insulin,
Phenergan 12.5 mg p.o. q.6 hours p.r.n. nausea, Protonix 40
mg 1 tab daily.



                        Justin Dana King, MD

Dictated By:Howard
